sentences.base_language: Jiyeon moved to Seoul three months ago for her new job.
sentences.target_language: 지연이는 새 직장 때문에 세 달 전에 서울로 이사했어요.
sentences.romanized: Jiyeoni-neun sae jikjang ttaemune se dal jeone Seoulloe isahaesseoyo.
sentences.literal: Jiyeon new job because-of three months ago Seoul-to moved.
sentences.grammarNote:
**때문에** (ttaemune) = 'because of' — attached to nouns: 직장 때문에 (because of work). For verbs, use -기 때문에.
**전에** (jeone) = 'ago / before': 세 달 전에 = 'three months ago.' Compare: 후에 (jeone) = 'after.'
**이사하다** (isahada) = 'to move house / relocate' — 이사 (移徙) + 하다. The destination particle 로 marks Seoul as the direction of the move: 서울로 이사하다 (move to Seoul).
**로/으로** here expresses direction toward a destination — a key use of this particle alongside 'means/method.'

sentences.base_language: Tonight she was sitting alone in her apartment feeling homesick.
sentences.target_language: 오늘 밤 혼자 아파트에 앉아서 고향이 그리웠어요.
sentences.romanized: Oneul bam honja apateu-e anjaseo gohyangi geuriwosseoyo.
sentences.literal: Tonight alone apartment-in sitting hometown missed.
sentences.grammarNote: -아/어서 (a/eoseo) here shows sequence or cause: 앉아서 = 'sitting and (then)...' or 'because (she) sat.' The connector links two actions where the first leads to or accompanies the second. Note: 앉다 has stem 앉-, which takes -아서 because the final vowel in the root is ㅏ → 앉아서.

sentences.base_language: She missed her mother's cooking most of all.
sentences.grammarNote:
**그립다** (geuripda) = 'to miss / to long for' — an irregular ㅂ adjective: 그리워요 (miss/long for), 그리운 (the missing/longed-for). 그리운 건 = 'the thing that is missed.'
**의** (eui) = possessive particle 'of/possessing': 엄마의 음식 = 'mom's food.' In everyday speech it's often reduced to just omitting it or pronouncing it as [에].
**제일** (jeil) = 'most / best' — here used as a superlative adverb: 제일 그리운 건 = 'the thing I miss most.'

sentences.base_language: She wanted to eat her mother's kimchi stew right now.
sentences.target_language: 지금 당장 엄마가 만든 김치찌개가 먹고 싶었어요.
sentences.romanized: Jigeum dangjang eommaga mandeun gimchijjigaega meokgo sipeosseoyo.
sentences.literal: Right now mom made kimchi-stew eat-want-was.
sentences.grammarNote: -고 싶다 (go sipda) = 'want to (do something).' Attach -고 싶다 to any verb stem: 먹고 싶다 (want to eat), 가고 싶다 (want to go), 보고 싶다 (want to see). In past tense: -고 싶었어요 (wanted to). 보고 싶어요 also means 'I miss you' — literally 'I want to see (you).'

sentences.base_language: She decided to call her mother.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-기로 하다** (-giro hada) = 'to decide to do': 전화하기로 했어요 = 'decided to call.'
This pattern nominalises the verb with -기, then adds 로 하다 (to do/go with). It expresses a resolved decision.
**에게** (ege) = 'to / for' when the recipient is a person or animal: 엄마에게 전화하다 = 'call mom' (literally 'make a call to mom').
**전화하다** (jeonhwahada) = 'to make a phone call' — 전화 (電話, telephone) + 하다. 전화를 걸다 (jeonhwareul geolda) is a more specific alternative meaning 'to dial/place a call.'

sentences.base_language: Mom! I miss you so much.
sentences.grammarNote:
**보고 싶다** (bogo sipda) = 'to miss (someone)' — literally 'want to see.' This is the standard Korean expression for missing a person: 엄마가 보고 싶어요 (I miss my mom).
**너무** intensifies the longing: 너무 보고 싶어요 = 'I miss you so much!'
엄마 (eomma) = mom — informal and affectionate, used in everyday speech. The more formal term is 어머니 (eomoeni).
보고 싶다 can also mean 'I want to see (a movie, etc.)' — context makes it clear which is meant.

sentences.base_language: Me too, sweetheart. How are you feeling?
sentences.target_language: 나도 그래, 얘야. 요즘 어떻게 지내니?
sentences.romanized: Nado geurae, yaeyа. Yojeum eotteoke jinaeni?
sentences.literal: Me-too so, dear. These-days how getting-along?
sentences.grammarNote:
**얘야** (yaeyа) = 'dear / sweetie' — a warm term a parent uses for their child. 얘 = a contracted form of 이 아이 (this child); 야 is the informal address particle used with names or nouns.
**나도** (nado) = 'me too' — 나 (I/me, informal) + 도 (also/too). Compare: 저도 (jeodo) = 'me too' (polite).
**-니?** is a casual informal question ending used by elders or parents to children: 지내니? = 'are you doing okay?' More intimate than 지내요?.

sentences.base_language: I'm okay, but I feel lonely because I'm far from home.
sentences.grammarNote: -아/어서 expressing cause: 멀어서 = 'because (it is) far.' The stem 멀다 (to be far) takes -어서 → 멀어서. This construction is very natural for explaining feelings: 피곤해서 (because tired), 바빠서 (because busy). The cause clause always comes before the result.

sentences.base_language: I think things will get better soon.
sentences.grammarNote: -겠어요 (gesseooyo) expresses conjecture or future resolve: 괜찮아지겠어요 = 'it will probably get better / I'm sure it will get better.' -겠- signals that the speaker is inferring or expressing intention. 알겠어요 (I understand / I'll understand), 가겠어요 (I will go), 힘들겠어요 (it must be hard).

sentences.base_language: Mom encouraged her and told her to eat well and sleep enough.
sentences.target_language: 엄마는 잘 먹고 잘 자라고 격려해 줬어요.
sentences.romanized: Eommaneun jal meokgo jal jarago gyeongnyeo hae jwosseoyo.
sentences.literal: Mom well eat-and well sleep-quote encouraged gave.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-라고** (-rago) is an imperative indirect speech ending: 잘 먹으라고 (telling to eat well), 잘 자라고 (telling to sleep well). It reports a command or instruction someone gave.
**격려하다** (gyeongnyeohada) = 'to encourage / cheer on' — a key emotional support verb.
**해 줬어요** = 'did (it) for (her)' — -아/어 주다 frames the action as a gift of support: 격려해 줬어요 = 'encouraged (her) (as a gesture of love).'
This sentence captures a quintessentially Korean parenting phrase: 잘 먹고 잘 자라 (eat well and sleep well).

sentences.base_language: After the call, Jiyeon felt a little warmer inside.
sentences.target_language: 전화를 끊고 나서 지연이는 마음이 조금 따뜻해졌어요.
sentences.romanized: Jeonhwareul kkeungo naseo Jiyeoni-neun maeumi jogeum ttatteuthaejyeosseoyo.
sentences.literal: Call hung-up after Jiyeon heart a-little warmed.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-고 나서** (-go naseo) = 'after doing' — emphasises that the first action is fully completed before the second begins: 끊고 나서 = 'after hanging up.'
**끊다** (kkeunta) = 'to hang up / cut off' — used for ending a phone call: 전화를 끊다 (hang up the phone).
**마음이 따뜻해지다** = 'to feel warmer inside / to feel one's heart warm' — 마음 (heart/mind) + 따뜻해지다 (become warm). A beautiful and common idiom for emotional warmth.

sentences.base_language: She made herself a cup of tea and opened her laptop to look for recipes.
sentences.target_language: 차를 한 잔 끓이고 레시피를 찾으려고 노트북을 열었어요.
sentences.romanized: Chareul han jan kkeureigo resipireul chajeuryeogo noteubug-eul yeoreosseoyo.
sentences.literal: Tea one cup boiled-and recipes search-in-order-to laptop opened.
sentences.grammarNote:
**끓이다** (kkeulida) = 'to boil / make (by boiling)' — the causative form of 끓다 (to boil intransitively): 차를 끓이다 (boil/make tea).
**-(으)려고** (-(eu)ryeogo) = 'in order to / with the intention of': 찾으려고 = 'in order to search for.' It expresses purpose or intent behind an action.
**열다** (yeolda) = 'to open' — past tense: 열었어요. Used for physical opening (a laptop, door) and metaphorical opening.
**노트북** (noteubuk) = laptop computer — a common loanword from English 'notebook (computer).'
